query: SMAD6 gene variants association with craniosynostosis

retrieved_docs.title: Scaphocephaly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: In non-syndromic cases of sagittal craniosynostosis, it is recommended to perform screening for  SMAD6 mutations, and if present, genetic counseling is given to the parents. [3] If there is no family history for craniosynostoses and no genetic mutations are found, there is a 5% recurrence risk to offsprings for nonsyndromic sagittal craniosynostosis. [71]

retrieved_docs.title: Scaphocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: A combination of external factors and genetic mutations is implicated in this condition's etiology. There have been more than 100 mutations associated with nonsyndromic craniosynostosis. The most common and most studied mutation is the fibroblast growth factors (FGFR) gene 1-3. [2] In sagittal craniosynostosis, the most common mutation is in the SMAD6 gene. [3] Other mutations associated are in the TWIST1, TCF12, ERF, MSX2 genes. [3] [4] [5] [6] However, only in a few cases, mutations are identified. [2] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Craniosynostosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Almost 20% of all craniosynostoses have a genetic basis, most of which are inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion. [2] Roughly 86% of cases involve a single-gene mutation, whereas 15% are linked to chromosomal abnormalities. [3] [4] The most common genes affected in craniosynostosis are those in the fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) pathway. FGFR-1 is associated with Pfeiffer syndrome, FGFR-2 with Apert, Crouzon, and type 1 Pfeiffer syndromes, and FGFR-3 with Muenke syndrome. [3] [5] Mutations in TWIST1 have been described in cases of Saethre-Chotzen syndrome. [3] [6] Although most cases with these mutations are found in syndromic cases, mutations in SMAD6, TCF12, ERF, and MSX2 have been associated with non-syndromic cases. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Screening for SMAD6 mutations is recommended in patients with nonsyndromic metopic craniosynostosis as its presence confers a high risk for recurrence in another pregnancy. [26] In those cases where the mutation is identified, genetic counseling is recommended. Head 3D CT scans are helpful as an educational tool for families to understand the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Radioulnar Synostosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of congenital radioulnar synotosis is unknown. Congenital radioulnar synostosis may be seen in Tetrasomy X or any number of genetic syndromes, including Poland, Cornelia de Lange, Holt-Oram, Crouzon, and Apert syndromes. Radioulnar synotosis may result from SMAD6-deficiency; SMAD6 encodes an intracellular inhibitor of the bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) signaling pathway. [1] [2] [3] The growing limb bud typically undergoes longitudinal segmentation at approximately 7 weeks of gestation; if this process fails to occur or does so abnormally, a congenital radioulnar synostosis may develop. [4] An autosomal dominant inheritance pattern of radioulnar synostosis has been hypothesized, but no clear cause or definitive inheritance pattern has been proven. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Rare non-syndromic cases have been described in which there is the fusion of both the metopic and the sagittal suture producing significant behavior and cognitive and motor impairment. [14] These patients require a combined surgical approach to treat both sutures. Thyroid dysfunction in mothers can contribute to metopic synostosis in children, next only to sagittal and coronal synostosis. Folic acid deficiency before pregnancy has also been proposed as a causative factor for trigonocephaly. [13] The most notable genetic mutations recently identified in non-syndromic metopic synostosis are FREM1 and SMAD6. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Craniosynostosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors: Around 15% of SSC cases involve causal variants in 29 genes, with mutations being autosomal dominant in 8% of cases. Gene associations vary by suture type and familial patterns.

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 6% to 10% of patients with trigonocephaly show genetic alterations. [30] Metopic craniosynostosis has been associated with several chromosomal abnormalities, including deletion of chromosome 11q24, 7p, 3q, 13q, 12pter, 22q11, deletion or trisomy of 9p, duplication of 15q25, and ERF and SMAD6 gene mutations. [13] [26] [31] [32] [33] Familial cases occur in approximately 5% of the cases, with an 8% frequency in twins. [34] [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Approaches to Congenital Aortic Stenosis -- Evaluation -- Familial Screening Recommendations
retrieved_docs.content: Certain hereditary thoracic aortic diseases show an increased prevalence of BAV. For instance, about 10% of patients with Loeys-Dietz syndrome, associated with pathogenic variants in genes such as TGFBR1 , TGFBR2 , SMAD3 , TGFB2 , and TGFB3 , have BAV. Hereditary thoracic aortic diseases are linked to pathogenic variants in NOTCH1 , ACTA2 , MAT2A , SMAD6 , and LOX , which also show a higher prevalence of BAV. [22] Notably, despite the familial nature of BAV and thoracic aortic aneurysms, most patients with these conditions will not have an identifiable pathogenic genetic variant even after genetic testing. However, when the condition is familial, patients with BAV and aortopathy should be evaluated, counseled, and genetically tested by a medical geneticist or a specialist in genetic aortopathy. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Craniosynostosis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If a syndromic case is suspected, genetic testing should be performed, particularly for FGFR gene mutations. Ongoing research continues to explore and identify the genetic causes of these syndromes. To date, 57 genes have been identified as having a relationship with craniosynostosis, the most common ones being those mentioned previously. [13] [31] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, studies have tried to find other genes that might have specific links to 46, XX gonadal dysgenesis, and the gene BMP15 appears to be a possible candidate. However, a definite link to gonadal dysgenesis remains unaffirmed. BMP15 is a gene that codes for proteins apart from the transforming growth factor-beta family made by oocytes. A rare amino acid substitution at p.A180T in BMP15 was present in a small number of patients with ovarian failure in several studies; additional studies have found that this amino acid substitution may be a rare polymorphism [13] .

retrieved_docs.title: Brachycephaly -- Etiology -- Brachycephaly Due to Craniosynostosis (Synostotic Brachycephaly)
retrieved_docs.content: Premature abnormal mineralization of the cranial sutures, which can occur between the calvaria bones, results in craniosynostosis. Bilateral premature mineralization of the coronal sutures leads to bi-coronal craniosynostosis, referred to as anterior brachycephaly. [10] Unilateral premature mineralization of the coronal suture in infants results in anterior plagiocephaly, a related skull deformity. Genetic abnormalities such as fibroblast growth factor receptor type-2 ( FGFR-2) and type-3 ( FGFR-3) , twist homolog-1 ( TWIST1) , and ephrin-B1 ( EFNB1) gene mutations may predispose an infant to craniosynostosis. [11] Fibroblast growth factor receptor type-2 plays an integral role in the abnormality, although the exact etiology remains unclear. Fibroblast growth factors (FGFs) participate in the differentiation, cell proliferation, migration, and regulation of normal bone morphogenesis. [12] These mutations may be clinically syndromic or non-syndromic. [10] Clinical syndromes with craniosynostosis include Crouzon syndrome, Apert syndrome, Pfeiffer syndrome, Saethre-Chotzen syndrome, Antley-Bixler syndrome, and Muenke syndrome. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Scaphocephaly -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Alterations in the expression of growth factors have been implicated in the pathophysiology of craniosynostosis. Various mutations have been identified in craniosynostosis, including three fibroblast growth factors and other genes such as the twisted family basic helix-loop-helix transcription factor TWIST 1. [4] [5] [12] [13] [14] They are more frequently found in syndromic craniosynostosis. [15] In sagittal craniosynostosis, approximately 10% of the patients show genetic alterations. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandibulofacial Dysostosis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Once a pathogenic variant has been identified in an affected family member, it is possible to offer prenatal and preimplantation genetic testing. [2] Affected individuals and their families are highly encouraged to seek genetic counseling. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Crouzon Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the potential for underdiagnosis in resource-limited settings, incidence rates remain consistent globally. The sporadic nature of the condition is highlighted by the fact that de novo mutations account for 50% to 60% of cases. [16] The age of diagnosis is contingent upon the severity of craniofacial abnormalities. Imaging and physical examination are commonly used to identify severe cases during pregnancy or at birth. Milder cases may remain undiagnosed until later in childhood when developmental delays or functional impairments become apparent. No gender bias has been observed. However, there is ongoing interest in studying the environmental and genetic factors that influence FGFR2 mutations. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Oculo Auriculo Vertebral Spectrum -- Etiology -- Genetic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Most commonly, OAVS is sporadic. [6] [7] However, family history has been reported in 2 to 12% of cases. The phenotypic characters are similar in both familial and sporadic types of OAVS. There is a 2 to 3% risk of OAVS in the first-degree relatives of an affected individual. Various chromosomal abnormalities have been identified. [2] These include: The 5p15.33-pter deletion (most common type) [8] [9] [10] Deletion of 12p13.33 region involving the WNT5B gene Partially overlapping microduplications on 14q23.1 Anomalies in 22q chromosome Anomalies in 14g32 containing GSC (GOOSECOID) gene Deletion of 14q31.1q31.3, a region adjacent to GSC gene Linkage to 15q26.2-q26.3 region Deletion of 12q13.33 Partial trisomy of the 22q11 region Aneuploidy in chromosome X Translocation t(9;12) (p23;q12.2) Mosaicism of trisomy 7, 9 and 22 Duplication of SIX1, SIX6, and OTX2

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: The qualifier “vrozené,” which denotes a congenital origin, can be applied to several disorders, indicating that they may be present from birth. It is an allowed qualifier for ناسور مَثانِيٌّ مَهْبِلِيّ, قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة, انسداد صفراوي خارج الكبد, فرط البروتينات الشحمية, and الْتِهابُ المثانة. Recognizing these conditions as congenital guides clinicians toward appropriate genetic evaluation, early diagnostic testing, and tailored management plans that address their lifelong nature.

retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is identified as a genetic factor (genetika) associated with several specific conditions, including اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (excessive tearing), and فَرْطُ ضَغْطِ الدَّم العيني (increased intra‑ocular pressure). Recognizing this genetic linkage can aid clinicians in understanding the hereditary contributions to these disorders and may inform diagnostic evaluation and personalized management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: congenital anomalies** contain hereditary and genetic disorders, which also comprise **other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions** and sexually transmitted infections; this hereditary‑genetic group is a member of both **فالج طفلي** and **متلازمة ستيكلر**. Collectively, the integrated structure demonstrates how endocrine, metabolic, fluid‑electrolyte, renal, infectious, vascular, otolaryngologic, behavioral, psychiatric, genetic, and developmental concepts intersect, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment, risk stratification, and multidisciplinary clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both included within the SMQ titled الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ), which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This SMQ itself is classified as a member of the broader category فجل طفلي, indicating that these conditions are considered part of the larger spectrum of childhood developmental disorders. The hierarchical relationship underscores how specific anomalies and infections are organized under a unified genetic and congenital disorder framework, which in turn is situated within the context of pediatric developmental health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1062
retrieved_docs.content: within nervous system disorders. Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.
